round-B_SeniorLead_reasoning: 摘要指出研究聚焦於精神健康領域中GloVe/Word2Vec等詞向量的偏見評估，並包含文獻回顧與嵌入向量實驗，雖屬原創研究但明確不涉及「自動化流程抽取（process extraction）」或提出/驗證任何流程抽取方法，因而不符合本次收錄主題；兩位初審給3（不確定），但摘要已呈現否定性證據，故評為可能排除。

Both junior reviewers gave 3 (uncertain) noting the paper studies BERT gender bias in emotion/sentiment tasks; after review the abstract clearly focuses on bias mitigation and sentiment/emotion regression, not on automated process extraction (the required topic), so it does not meet the inclusion criteria for NLP-for-process-extraction and should be excluded.

Abstract and title focus on LLM security, a deep CNN-based security framework evaluated on the KDDCup dataset, and themes like cybersecurity and data privacy; there is no mention of automated process extraction, methods for converting natural-language descriptions into structured process models, or experiments validating such a process-extraction method. Junior reviewers likewise flagged the mismatch. Lacking explicit evidence that the paper addresses NLP-for-process-extraction, I score it as uncertain and needing the full text to confirm.
摘要描述LLM自我協作以生成程式碼（analyst/coder/tester），重點在軟體開發與程式碼生成，並未討論將自然語言的流程描述轉換為結構化流程模型或以NLP進行流程抽取，故不符合本場域的主題要求（缺少NLP-for-process-extraction的任務陳述與相關實驗證據）。兩位初審給3分表示不確定，但基於摘要明確主題不符，我判定排除。
摘要描述一個以 LLM 驅動的多代理軟體開發（設計/編碼/測試）對話框架，並未提及將自然語言轉換為結構化流程模型、也未呈現針對流程抽取的具體 NLP 方法或實驗驗證；兩位初審給予保守的 3 分，我認為與“NLP for process extraction”主題不符，傾向排除。

The title and abstract explicitly describe this work as a survey of LLM-based multi-agent systems (secondary research) and do not report an original NLP-for-process-extraction method or empirical validation, so it fails the primary-research and topical inclusion criteria.

BERT is a foundational general-purpose NLP pre-training paper showing strong results on benchmarks (GLUE, MultiNLI, SQuAD), but the title/abstract do not mention automated process extraction or any experiments on process-extraction tasks—junior reviewers also rated it 3—so without full-text evidence of a concrete process-extraction method and experiments I cannot include it and recommend a score of 3 (need full text to confirm relevance).

The title and abstract clearly focus on LLM prompting for code generation (AceCoder) and report code-generation benchmarks, with no mention of transforming natural-language descriptions into structured process models (automated process extraction); both junior reviewers flagged relevance concerns (gave 3), but the abstract provides explicit negative evidence, so exclude.
摘要描述的是基於草圖的自動程式碼生成（檢索相似程式碼、提取並編輯代碼 sketch）並報告模型與資料集實驗，沒有提到將自然語言描述轉換為流程/活動/控制流或過程模型的內容，與「自動化流程抽取」主題不符；兩位初審給3分表示不確定，但此為明顯的主題不匹配，故給予強烈排除。

The abstract describes improving NL-to-code/program synthesis (retrieval + constrained decoding) evaluated on SQL, Vega-Lite and SMCalFlow and contains no indication of extracting activities/actors/control-flow into structured process models, so it does not meet the 'NLP for process extraction' inclusion criterion and should likely be excluded (score 2); junior reviewers also noted uncertainty but found no process-extraction evidence.

Junior reviewers disagreed (one gave 3 citing relevance uncertainty; one gave 5 citing clear method+experiments). The abstract describes a concrete retrieval-augmented generative QA method for event argument extraction and reports empirical validation across settings, so it meets the ‘‘concrete method’’ and ‘‘experiments’’ inclusion criteria; however, it does not explicitly state that it targets automated process extraction (i.e., transforming natural-language descriptions of activities/actors/control flow into structured process models), so its topical relevance is uncertain and requires inspecting the full text. Therefore I refrain from a hard include/exclude and request full-text review.

The abstract clearly focuses on using LLMs for GPU/OpenCL performance modeling rather than on NLP-based automated process extraction from natural-language text, so it fails the primary topical inclusion criterion (no evidence of methods or experiments for extracting process models from text); junior reviewers similarly noted topic mismatch.

The abstract explicitly frames the paper as a review of LLMs in process mining—discussing capabilities, benchmark strategies, and evaluation—without proposing a concrete NLP-for-process-extraction method or reporting empirical experiments, so it fits the exclusion criterion of secondary research and should be excluded.

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract presents a declarative workflow formalism (DCR Graphs), a hospital example, and a mapping to Büchi automata but does not mention any NLP-based process extraction, concrete NLP methods, or empirical validation, so I rate it 3 (needs full text to determine relevance).

The title and abstract describe a concrete, original contribution—an NLP-augmented tool (Process Highlighter) for creating declarative DCR process models from natural language and report empirical observations in applied settings—so the topic and method appear relevant; however the abstract does not clearly report concrete experimental design, quantitative evaluation, or confirm full-paper availability (junior reviewers gave 3 and 5), so full-text inspection is needed to verify empirical validation and methodological details.

The abstract presents a declarative workflow framework (Declare) with process-mining diagnostics but contains no mention of NLP-based process extraction from natural-language text, a concrete extraction method, or empirical validation—both junior reviewers raised the same lack of evidence—so inclusion is uncertain and needs the full text to confirm.

Title and abstract describe a qualitative thematic analysis in nursing (inductive/deductive coding, phenomenology) with no mention of NLP, automated process extraction, a concrete NLP method, or empirical validation experiments; both junior reviewers flagged the same absence—thus it likely does not meet the NLP-for-process-extraction inclusion criteria, so I recommend possible exclusion.

The title and abstract describe an original English-language study that applies pre-trained LMs (GPT-3) with in-context learning to extract business process entities/relations and reports empirical experiments/results (e.g., activities, participants, performs relation), satisfying the inclusion criteria—one junior reviewer flagged uncertainty about full-text availability, but no negative evidence is present, so I recommend strong inclusion.
摘要主要是介紹開放原始碼的 GATE 工具及其在生醫領域的應用案例與生命周期支援，但並未明確提出針對「自動化流程抽取」的具體方法或實驗驗證（缺少方法細節與實證實驗的證據），因此暫難判定是否符合收錄標準。
標題與摘要描述的 ON-LSTM 聚焦於語言模型、無監督句法解析、語法評估與邏輯推理，未提及將 NLP 用於自動化流程抽取或相關實驗，且兩位初審者亦認為主題相關性不足，因此傾向排除。

The title/abstract describe a general IE contribution—uneven-margin SVM/Perceptron to handle imbalanced training and validated on IE benchmark corpora—which could be relevant but do not explicitly mention automated process extraction (no reference to extracting activities/actors/control flow, process models, or process-specific datasets); both junior reviewers also judged it uncertain (3). Missing evidence: explicit focus on NLP-for-process-extraction, process-structured outputs, or experiments on process extraction corpora—full text needed to confirm relevance.

Title/abstract describe a comparison of LSTM and BERT for intent classification on a small chatbot dataset, which is explicitly about intent classification rather than automated process extraction and thus lacks evidence of addressing the targeted task or methods for process extraction (junior reviewers noted uncertainty), so it is likely to be excluded at this stage.

The abstract (and both junior reviews) show this is a paper about general word-vector (GloVe) modeling and its evaluation (analogies, similarity, NER) with no mention of transforming natural-language descriptions into structured process models or any process-extraction method/experiments, so it does not meet the NLP-for-process-extraction topic requirement.
題目與摘要聚焦於BERT的蒸餾與模型縮減（DistilBERT），未提及或展示將此模型用於自動化流程抽取的具體方法或實驗；兩位初審皆評為3分，缺少針對「NLP for process extraction」的明確證據，故在 Stage 1 採保留待全文以獲更多資訊（評分3）。

The abstract focuses on improving Skip-gram word and phrase vector learning (subsampling, negative sampling, phrase detection) and does not mention applying these techniques to automated process extraction, nor does it describe a concrete method for transforming natural language into process models or experiments validating such an application—therefore, it lacks the key evidence required for inclusion but should not be excluded at this high-recall stage. Missing evidence: explicit NLP-for-process-extraction application, a concrete process-extraction method, and empirical evaluation for that task.

Both junior reviewers rated this as uncertain (3); the abstract describes an entity-level relation extraction model (DocRED, coreference, multi-instance learning) and does not mention extracting activities, actors, control flow, or transforming text into process models — therefore it appears outside the NLP-for-process-extraction scope and should likely be excluded.
摘要明確描述 CatBoost 的梯度提升與類別特徵處理，屬於一般機器學習/模型工程工作且未涉及自然語言處理或自動化流程抽取，故不符合本徵選標準。

Title and abstract describe an algorithmic approach to measure consistency between process models using behavioral profiles and Petri nets (with experiments on SAP models), but provide no indication of NLP methods or extraction from natural-language text (missing evidence of natural-language input, NLP techniques, datasets, or text-based experiments); therefore keep as uncertain pending full-text confirmation.
摘要明確談論對既有商業流程模型的相似度度量與比較（節點、結構、行為相似度）並以精確率/召回率評估，但未提及任何從自然語言文本自動抽取流程的 NLP 方法或相關實驗，因而不符合「NLP-for-process-extraction」的收錄標準，傾向排除。

Junior reviewers both rated 3; the abstract describes a general pre-trained language model (BERT) with SOTA results on common NLP benchmarks but gives no indication it specifically addresses automated process extraction or reports methods/experiments for that task, so evidence is insufficient to include at this stage.
摘要描述以隱馬可夫模型與決策規則從手動執行的流程跡跡（事件/執行痕跡）歸納工作流程結構，未提及處理自然語言或使用 NLP 方法，因此很可能不符合「從自然語言文本抽取流程」的納入標準（缺少任何 NLP/自然語言輸入的證據）。
兩位初審均指出摘要不是研究論文；標題為 Deloitte Insights 類產業文章，摘要僅為作者簡介，完全缺乏原創研究、具體方法或實驗驗證的證據，亦非聚焦於以 NLP 做流程擷取；依據這些明確否定性證據，應排除。

I reviewed the title/abstract and the two junior reviews (both rated 3); the paper is topically relevant—discussing NLP-based textual process discovery and legal/practitioner challenges—but the abstract does not specify a concrete NLP method, experimental validation, or confirm full-paper/full-text availability, so inclusion cannot be confirmed from title+abstract alone. One-sentence explanation: Relevant topic but missing key evidence (method, experiments, full-text) so mark as 3 (uncertain / needs more evidence).

The abstract explicitly states this is a "position paper" that outlines high-level challenges and selected applications and does not present a concrete NLP-for-process-extraction method or empirical experiments (both required by inclusion criteria); junior reviewers rated it 3 for uncertainty, but the explicit designation as a position paper is decisive for exclusion.
摘要明確指出本文以質性分析檢視現有文獻並測試參考工具，未宣稱提出具體且可驗證的原創 NLP 方法或實作細節，較像二次研究/工具評估而非符合「原創方法與實驗驗證」的納入準則，故傾向排除。

The abstract explicitly frames the paper as a state-of-the-art overview/literature analysis rather than proposing a concrete, novel text-to-model method or reporting experiments, so it fails the inclusion requirement for primary research with empirical validation; the two junior reviewers flagged uncertainty but also noted it reads like a review. Given this clear evidence that the submission is secondary research, I recommend exclusion.

Although one junior reviewer labeled it as a literature review, the abstract explicitly describes a systematic, quantitative comparison of 10 state-of-the-art approaches, an evaluation pipeline on a unifying task with dataset/metrics, and reflections on limitations—indicating original empirical work and experiments suitable for full review.

The abstract describes BERT, a general and highly effective pre-trained NLP model with SOTA results on standard benchmarks but does not mention automated process extraction, any concrete method or experiments for that task (both junior reviewers also rated 3); therefore evidence is insufficient to confirm relevance to process-extraction and more full-text evidence is needed.

The abstract explicitly frames the submission as a position paper proposing a research agenda (not an original empirical study) and contains no indication of a concrete method, experiments, or primary empirical results required by the inclusion criteria, so it should be excluded.

Title and abstract describe a methodological guideline/report for conducting systematic reviews in software engineering (secondary work), with no mention of NLP-based process extraction, concrete methods, or experiments to validate any method—therefore it does not meet the inclusion criteria.

The title and abstract present a concrete framework (Text2DMN) using NLP, rule-based and ML techniques to extract decision models from natural-language text—clearly relevant to NLP-for-process-extraction—but the abstract does not mention empirical experiments or validation (a key inclusion criterion), so given the recall-oriented stage I recommend forwarding for full review to check for experiments and full-text details.

The abstract describes an original NLP-based approach to extract business process models from user stories (including concrete implementation details: Prolog rules integrated into Python) and reports experimental evaluation on use cases, which matches the inclusion criteria for primary research on NLP-for-process-extraction and provides no negative evidence about paper status or full text availability.

Both junior reviews noted the paper presents a bidirectional synchronization framework (generating textual work instructions from BPMN and updating models when texts are edited) and reports experiments showing equivalence, so it is potentially relevant; however the abstract does not explicitly describe concrete NLP methods for extracting process structure from arbitrary natural-language text (e.g., algorithms, parsing/NER/semantic mapping techniques), nor details on text constraints, datasets, or evaluation metrics—therefore uncertainty remains and more evidence from the full paper is needed.

The abstract describes a concrete method and graphical tool combining Group Storytelling with Text Mining/NLP (relevant to NLP-for-process-extraction) but gives no explicit evidence of experiments or empirical validation or confirmation of full-paper availability—both junior reviewers flagged this uncertainty—so more information (full text) is needed before inclusion.

Both junior reviewers rated this as uncertain (3). The abstract explicitly calls it a "Doctoral Consortium paper," which is typically not a full research article; it also only describes a high-level idea (deriving BPMN elements from sentence syntax) and gives no concrete method description, full-text availability, or empirical validation/experiments—all required by the inclusion criteria. Missing evidence: confirmation it is a full research paper, a detailed method, and experimental evaluation.

The abstract clearly introduces PET, a new annotated dataset for extracting activities/gateways/actors/flow from business process text and mentions baselines (directly relevant to NLP-based process extraction), but it does not confirm full-paper/full-text availability or provide explicit details of methods and experimental validation—junior reviewers scored 4 and 3, so due to missing confirmation I rate this as uncertain and request full-text for final inclusion.

The abstract presents an original PME contribution (a hierarchical neural network framing PME as multi-grained text classification with a coarse-to-fine training mechanism), reports two constructed datasets and statistically significant experimental results, and contains no negative evidence about paper type or full text availability—therefore I recommend strong inclusion.

Abstract presents a concrete NLP-based method (spreadsheet intermediate representation, semantic analysis, transformation to BPMN) so the topic and primary contribution match inclusion, but it does not mention any experiments or full-text availability required for acceptance—junior reviewers also rated it 3—so retain for full review pending those details.

The title and abstract describe an updated reporting guideline (PRISMA 2020) with checklists and flow diagrams—a secondary reporting/methods guidance piece—so it is not primary research on NLP process extraction, does not present a concrete NLP method or experiments, and both junior reviewers flagged it for exclusion.

The abstract describes a concrete NLP algorithm (implemented in the Visual Narrator tool) that automatically derives conceptual models from user stories and reports empirical evaluation with precision/recall (80–92%) across two case studies and positive user feedback, which satisfies several inclusion criteria (original method, experiments); however, it is ambiguous whether the task is specifically ‘process extraction’ (activities/actors/control flow) rather than broader conceptual/domain modeling, and full-text availability is not explicitly stated—given the recall-oriented stage and mixed junior reviewer opinions, I recommend passing to full review for confirmation. (One-sentence explanation: include because the paper presents a concrete NLP method with empirical validation, but uncertain if it exactly fits “process extraction”.)

Title indicates a relevant case study on using LLMs for process mining (abstractions, scenarios, prompt definitions), aligning with NLP-for-process-extraction, and both junior reviewers also flagged potential relevance; however the abstract is truncated and provides no concrete method, experimental details, or confirmation of full-paper status—so key inclusion criteria (concrete method and empirical validation) are missing from the given text. I therefore give a cautious keep (3): relevant but insufficient evidence—need full abstract/full text and details of methods/experiments to justify inclusion.

Both junior reviewers rated this as a vision/concept piece; the abstract proposes a "Large Process Model" combining LLMs and knowledge-based systems but does not describe a concrete NLP method for extracting processes from natural-language text nor report experiments or empirical validation—so evidence for meeting the inclusion criteria is insufficient but not explicitly negative. Missing: explicit NLP-for-process-extraction approach, methodological details, and experimental evaluation. Recommend keeping in flow (recall) for full-text check.

The abstract explicitly reports applying a specific LLM to three NLP-for-BPM tasks (imperative and declarative model mining, RPA-suitability assessment) and claims empirical comparisons showing comparable/better performance without heavy prompt engineering—this indicates primary research with experiments, so despite one junior reviewer’s caution I recommend strong inclusion.

Title suggests a broad discussion of LLMs applied to Business Process Management and the abstract is truncated and does not mention any concrete NLP method, process-extraction focus, or experiments; both junior reviewers flagged insufficient evidence—thus insufficient to confirm inclusion but not clearly excludable without full abstract/full text.
論文題目與摘要僅討論DMN決策模型的複雜度度量與實證評估，未提及使用自然語言處理從自然語言文本抽取流程、也未描述具體NLP方法或相關實驗；兩位初審亦指出相同問題，因此傾向可能排除。
